scenario: Junction Telco runs a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that handles 3800 jobs per day. A developer adds a method annotated @JobWorker without setting the type attribute, and another method annotated @JobWorker(type = "foo"). A reviewer asks which job type each method binds to so that operators can map them to BPMN service-task definitions in the process model.
question: Which job type does each method bind to under the documented @JobWorker rules?

options.text: The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute binds to the method name foo via the documented fallback rule
The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute binds to the simple class name of the enclosing Spring bean by default
The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute binds to a synthetic UUID generated by the starter for that method
The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute fails fast at startup with a missing-type configuration error

a.text: Correct. The docs state that with @JobWorker(type = "foo") the worker handles jobs of type foo, and when type is omitted the method name is used by default, so @JobWorker on a method named foo also handles type foo.

b.text: Incorrect. The fallback when type is omitted is the method name, not the simple class name of the bean; class-name binding is not the documented fallback rule.

c.text: Incorrect. The starter does not generate a synthetic UUID job type; it falls back to the method name when type is omitted from the annotation.

d.text: Incorrect. The starter does not fail at startup when type is omitted; it uses the method name as the job type per the documented default rule.

explanation: The starter binds @JobWorker(type = "foo") to job type foo. When the type attribute is omitted, the method name is used by default as the job type, so a method named foo annotated @JobWorker also handles jobs of type foo.
